sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=63, col=7).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 171, level 2.
Valid actions right now: LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'LEFT': 'Moves player left ~6 columns', 'RIGHT': 'Moves player right ~6 columns', 'MOUSE': 'Clicks on position. Clicking green blocks collects them but game over after 8-9 total green clicks. Clicking dots or purple blocks adds 1 purple pixel to bottom bar. Player movement (LEFT/RIGHT) does NOT reset game over counter.', 'ACTION7': "Invalid action - gives 'Unknown action' error despite being in valid_actions list"}
- Object taxonomy: {'background': 'black with gray dots', 'green_blocks': 'light green 21-pixel blocks in 2x7 grid at rows 25-30 and 31-36, cols 13-52', 'purple_blocks_top': '3 purple blocks at rows 7-10, cols 14, 20, 26', 'dots': 'yellow/white dots at row 11 below purple blocks - clicking adds purple pixels to bottom bar', 'player': 'blue+yellow small object', 'platforms': 'large sky blue regions'}
- HUD map: {'bottom_bar': 'purple+white at row 63, resets on game over. Filling to 9 does NOT unlock green collection.'}
- Win pattern: {'level1': 'Collect green blocks with MOUSE, move LEFT toward pink cross to complete level', 'level2': 'UNKNOWN - all attempts to collect 14 green blocks fail with game over after 8-9 clicks'}
- Level log: {'level1': 'Completed in 50 actions - collect greens with MOUSE, approach pink cross, LEFT to complete', 'level2': 'Multiple game overs. All strategies failed: batch clicks, bar filling, interleaving with dots, player movement between clicks. Need fundamentally different approach.'}
- Cross-level notes: Level 2: Game over triggers after 8-9 green block clicks regardless of bar state, dot interleaving, or player movement. ACTION7 is invalid. The solution must involve a mechanic not yet discovered - possibly clicking non-green objects, using the player character differently, or a specific sequence/order.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 120 actions; a strong score needs about 48 or fewer. You are 2.5x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Early bar had 0 nodes at row 63 - meaning the bar didn't exist at the start of the game! The bar was created by my clicks. So the 14 purple pixels accumulated from my clicks, not from the initial state.
Wait, that changes everything. The bar starts empty and each click adds 1 purple pixel. I've made many clicks and now have 14 purple pixels. The bar is NOT a progress indicator for green blocks - it's just accumulating from clicks.
But 14 purple pixels = 14 green blocks... that could still be meaningful. Maybe I need exactly 14 purple pixels in the bar before something happens?
Let me think about what I haven't tried:
1. Clicking green blocks in a specific order
2. Moving the player to specific positions before clicking
3. Clicking the white portion of the bar
4. Some combination of actions
Actually, let me reconsider the game over mechanic. What if the game over counter is tied to the bar? What if having too many purple pixels causes game over when clicking greens? Or what if the bar needs to be at a specific value?
Wait - I just realized something. The early bar had 0 nodes at row 63. But the very first frame of the game (history[0]) might not have the bar. Let me check when the bar first appeared.
Actually, more importantly: what if the bar needs to have EXACTLY 14 purple pixels (one per green block) and then I click all 14 greens? But I can only click 8-9 before game over...
Unless the bar being at 14 changes the game over behavior! Let me test this. The bar currently has 14 purple pixels. Let me try clicking a green block now.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Bar has 14 purple pixels = 14 green blocks!
# Early frame had NO bar - bar accumulates from clicks
# Hypothesis: bar at 14 (matching green count) might unlock green collection
# Test: click a green block NOW with bar at 14

# Check current bar state first
seg = current_frame.segmentation
bar = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'p' and n['boundary'][0][0] == 63]
print(f"Bar pixels: {[n['pixels'] for n in bar]}")

# Click a green block - top row, first one (around row 27, col 15)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 27, 'col': 15}])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"ACTION7": "Invalid", "LEFT": "Moves player left ~6 columns", "MOUSE": "Any click adds 1 purple to bar. Green: game over persists.", "RIGHT": "Moves player right ~6 columns"}, "cross_level_notes": "Bar started empty, now has 14 purple = 14 greens. Testing if bar=14 unlocks green collection.", "hud_map": {"bottom_bar": "14 purple pixels, accumulates from clicks"}, "level_log": {"level2": "Testing green click with bar=14"}, "win_pattern": {"level1": "Collect green blocks, move LEFT toward pink cross"}}, "level_tier": {"current_plan": "Test green click with bar at 14", "failed_probes": ["Green collection without bar, LEFT, platform clicks, bar clicks"], "goal_model": "Bar at 14 may unlock green collection", "recent_findings": ["Bar starts empty, accumulates from clicks, now at 14"], "world_model": "14 greens, bar 14/64 purple"}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
